title: Tar on Cotton
stainChemistry: Tar is bitumen — a complex mixture of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, resins, and heavy petroleum fractions. Fresh tar is sticky and tenacious; aged tar hardens as volatile fractions evaporate. Tar bonds to cotton through strong hydrophobic interactions and mechanical adhesion — water does nothing, and surfactant alone is slow. Tar needs a like-dissolves-like approach: petroleum solvent. Cotton tolerates POG, mineral spirits, and kerosene at room temp without damage.
whyThisWorks: Mechanical removal first (while sticky tar is cool and scrape-able, or after chilling it brittle). Petroleum solvent dissolves the bitumen's aromatic + resin fractions. NSD emulsifies the dispersed tar for water rinse. Multiple passes are expected — tar plateaus slowly, lifting another layer each pass. The real home-treatment enemy is the user's instinct to scrub (which spreads tar) or to reach for water-based cleaners (which don't touch it).
defaultAssumption: Tar on cotton is rarely caught fresh-fresh — typically discovered hours after exposure (parking lot, road construction, beach tar). Expect 3–5 solvent cycles. Fabric damage is low with standard solvents; the challenge is the stain's tenacity, not fiber risk.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, ice pack (for brittle-lift), outdoor or ventilated space
spottingProtocol.instruction: SAFETY FORK: tar chemistry requires petroleum solvent. Solvent = flammable. Work outdoors or in a well-ventilated space. No open flame, no smoking, no lit stove nearby. Put on nitrile gloves. Then: if the tar is sticky, chill it brittle with an ice pack in a plastic bag (10–15 min direct contact). Lift off the hardened tar with a dull blade — flakes fracture cleanly. For fresh sticky tar, scrape as much as you can before chilling; ice ruins the sticky phase, lets the rest off easier.
Place a catch cloth UNDER the stain. When solvent mobilizes tar, it transfers DOWN, not sideways.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG, mineral spirits, OR kerosene on clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply petroleum solvent to a clean white cloth. Tamp from the BACK of the fabric. Flip cloth frequently — tar transfers out slowly. Expect 3–5 rounds. Never pour solvent directly. Patience — tar is a patient-removal stain.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD or Dawn in warm water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply generous NSD / Dawn, work with a soft brush for 2–3 min. Dawn is the household-grade solvent-cleanup — breaks up residual tar that the petroleum solvent freed but didn't carry away. Rinse cool. Repeat solvent → Dawn cycle once if shadow remains.
Launder hot per care label — but separately from other clothes in case residual tar lifts during the cycle. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Residual tar cures into a near-permanent film under dryer heat.

steps: 1. SAFETY: ventilation + gloves + no flame. Chill sticky tar brittle, scrape off bulk.
3. POG / mineral spirits tamp from back. 3–5 passes. Flip cloth.
4. NSD / Dawn to emulsify residual. Soft brush.
5. Hot wash per care label, separately from other items. Inspect before dryer.
products: Ice pack (for brittle-lift on sticky tar)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry (separate load)
warnings: FLAMMABLE — ventilation, gloves, no open flame
Never pour solvent directly on fabric
Never scrub sticky tar — spreads and embeds
Wash separately — residual tar can transfer to other items
Inspect before dryer — heat cures tar permanently

homeSolutions.instruction: Tar needs a petroleum solvent to dissolve, and petroleum solvents are FLAMMABLE. Work outside or in a well-ventilated garage, NOT in a bathroom or laundry room with a water heater pilot light nearby. Gloves on (nitrile, not latex). No smoking, no candles, no cooking nearby. Read this BEFORE you start.

If the tar is sticky and soft, scrape off as much as you can with a dull butter knife or credit-card edge. Don't rub — scraping lifts, rubbing spreads. If the tar is dried, put an ice pack (inside a plastic bag) directly on it for 10 minutes. Frozen tar gets brittle and chips off.

homeSolutions.agent: Place a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and put it under the stained area. When you dissolve the tar, it transfers DOWN into the catch cloth.

homeSolutions.agent: Mineral Spirits on a Clean White Rag
homeSolutions.instruction: Pour a little mineral spirits (paint thinner, from any hardware store) onto a clean white rag — NEVER directly on the fabric. Press the rag onto the tar from the BACK of the garment. Flip the rag to a clean spot every few seconds. Tar transfers out slowly — expect 3–5 passes. Some sources suggest WD-40 as a home alternative, and it does work, but it's also flammable and leaves a stronger residue that needs more Dawn cleanup.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Mineral spirits are flammable. Ventilate. No flames. Keep away from ignition sources.

homeSolutions.instruction: Work a GENEROUS amount of Dawn dish soap into the area with a soft toothbrush — more than you'd use on most stains. Dawn is what wildlife rehabbers use on oil-spill birds; it handles petroleum residue better than any other home product. Work it in for 2–3 minutes, let sit 5 minutes, rinse cool. Repeat if color or sheen remains.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash Separately, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder hot per care label, BY ITSELF (not mixed with other laundry — residual tar can transfer). Inspect BEFORE the dryer. Any remaining tar cures into a near-permanent film under dryer heat.

If two solvent + Dawn rounds haven't cleared the tar, take to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Tar on cotton, age [fresh / old], home-treated with mineral spirits + Dawn. [Hot-washed separately / not yet laundered]. [Fabric intact]."

steps: 1. SAFETY: ventilate, gloves, NO FLAME. Petroleum solvent is flammable.
2. Scrape / chill-brittle-and-flake as much tar as possible first.
4. Mineral spirits on a clean white RAG, tamp from back. 3–5 passes. Flip rag.
5. Dawn (generous) + soft brush + rinse. Repeat if needed.
6. Hot wash per care label, SEPARATELY from other laundry. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if two home rounds haven't cleared the tar, if the tar has been through a hot dryer and cured, OR if the fabric is starting to show solvent damage (rare on cotton but possible on blends).
warnings: MINERAL SPIRITS ARE FLAMMABLE — ventilate, no flames
Never pour solvent on fabric — tamp with cloth
Never scrub sticky tar — spreads it
Inspect BEFORE the dryer — heat cures tar permanently

neverDo: NEVER work with mineral spirits near open flame / water heater pilot / stove
Never pour solvent on cotton — tamp with cloth
Never mix laundry loads — tar transfers
Never hot-dry residual tar — cures permanently
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates POG, mineral spirits, kerosene, IPA, NSD, hot wash
Dyed cotton: solvent usually safe; inside-seam-test if concerned

materialWarnings: Dryer cures residual tar into a permanent film

escalation.when: Two home rounds haven't cleared tar, tar has been hot-dried and cured, or fabric shows unexpected solvent interaction.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tar on cotton, age [fresh / old], home-treated with mineral spirits + Dawn. [Hot-washed separately / not yet laundered]. Residual [size]."

contextQuestions: Is the tar fresh (sticky) or old (hardened)?
Road tar, roofing tar, or beach tar? (roofing has higher-MW hydrocarbons, harder)
Has it been through a dryer yet?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Tar is the petroleum solvent's home ground — like dissolves like. Safety posture (ventilation + no flame) is as important as the chemistry. Hot-dried tar is typically not recoverable.
